questions.question_text: Biomechanics in physical education is best defined as the scientific study of which one of the following aspects of human activity?

options.text: Mechanical principles applied to living organisms and human movement
Chemical reactions in the digestive system after meals
Social behaviour patterns in team sports settings
Marketing strategies for sporting goods companies

questions.explanation: Biomechanics is the application of mechanical principles — forces, motion, leverage and equilibrium — to the structure and function of living organisms; in sport it analyses how the human body produces and resists movement to improve performance and prevent injury.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: Digestive chemistry belongs to biochemistry, not biomechanics.
Social behaviour analysis is the field of sport sociology.
Marketing strategy lies outside the scope of biomechanics.

questions.question_text: The body movement called flexion in human anatomy is best described as which one of the following changes at a joint during movement?

options.text: Movement that increases the angle between two bones
Movement that decreases the angle between two bones
Movement of a bone away from the body's midline
Rotation of a bone around its own long axis

questions.explanation: Flexion is a body movement that decreases the angle between two articulating bones at a joint, such as bending the elbow to bring the forearm closer to the upper arm or curling the knee during a kick.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: Increasing the angle between bones is extension.
Movement away from midline is abduction.
Rotation around long axis is rotational movement.

questions.question_text: Which of the following best distinguishes abduction from adduction in the context of body movements occurring at a joint of the human body?

options.text: Abduction moves a part toward midline; adduction moves away
Abduction moves a part away from midline; adduction moves toward midline
Both abduction and adduction describe rotation around the long axis
Both terms describe straight-line forward movement only

questions.explanation: Abduction moves a body segment away from the midline of the body, such as raising the arm sideways; adduction moves it back toward the midline; the easy memory aid is 'add' = bring toward, 'ab' = away from.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: The directions are reversed in option A.
Rotation around long axis is a separate movement type.
Forward straight-line motion is not the abduction definition.

questions.question_text: Which of the following anatomical planes divides the human body vertically into the right and left halves during standing position analysis?

options.text: Sagittal plane
Frontal (coronal) plane
Transverse (horizontal) plane
Oblique plane

questions.explanation: The sagittal plane is a vertical plane running from front to back that divides the body into right and left halves; flexion and extension movements such as a forward kick or sit-up occur within this plane around a transverse axis.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: The frontal plane divides body into front and back halves.
The transverse plane divides into upper and lower portions.
Oblique planes are tilted, not strictly vertical.

questions.question_text: Which body movement primarily takes place in the frontal (coronal) plane around an anteroposterior axis during a simple side-to-side activity in sport?

options.text: Knee flexion during a forward kick
Arm abduction during a jumping-jack movement
Trunk rotation during a discus throw
Walking forward in a straight line on level ground

questions.explanation: Movements in the frontal plane around an anteroposterior (front-back) axis include abduction and adduction; raising the arms and legs sideways during a jumping-jack is a clear classroom example of frontal-plane movement in sport activity.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: Knee flexion in kicking is a sagittal-plane movement.
Trunk rotation in discus is a transverse-plane movement.
Walking forward involves mainly sagittal-plane action.

questions.question_text: The centre of gravity of a normal adult standing in the anatomical position is located most closely at which of the following body locations?

options.text: Approximately at the level of the second sacral vertebra (S2)
Just below the chin in the upper neck region
Inside the lower border of the right knee joint
At the very tip of the dominant index finger only

questions.explanation: In a standing adult in anatomical position, the centre of gravity lies inside the pelvis at about the level of the second sacral vertebra (S2), slightly anterior to the spine; its exact location shifts when the limbs move out of this neutral posture.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: The chin region is far above the body's mass distribution.
The knee lies well below the centre of mass.
The fingertip lies far outside the body's mass core.

questions.question_text: Which of the following postural changes is most likely to increase the stability of a wrestler preparing to resist a strong horizontal push from an opponent?

options.text: Standing very tall with feet placed close together
Lowering the centre of gravity and widening the stance
Lifting one leg off the ground entirely while shrugging
Bending forward only at the upper neck region

questions.explanation: Stability rises when the centre of gravity is lower, the base of support is larger, and the line of gravity falls inside the base; widening the stance and lowering the body satisfies all three conditions, making the wrestler harder to push over.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: Tall stance with narrow feet reduces base of support.
Lifting one leg shrinks the base of support sharply.
Bending only the neck does not affect overall stability.

questions.question_text: Which class of lever is best represented by a typical action of nodding the head forward at the atlanto-occipital joint of the cervical spine?

options.text: First-class lever (fulcrum between effort and load)
Second-class lever (load between effort and fulcrum)
Third-class lever (effort between fulcrum and load)
Fourth-class lever, exclusive to mammals

questions.explanation: Nodding at the atlanto-occipital joint is a classical first-class lever: the fulcrum is the joint between the skull and atlas, the load is the weight of the face anteriorly, and the effort is provided by the posterior neck extensor muscles.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: Standing on tip-toe is a typical second-class lever.
Biceps curl is a typical third-class lever in the body.
The fourth-class lever is not part of standard classification.

questions.question_text: Which of the following human movements provides the best example of a third-class lever system that is most common in body biomechanics?

options.text: Standing on tip-toe and rising on the balls of the feet
Nodding the head forward and backward at the neck joint
Flexing the elbow during a biceps curl with a dumbbell
Pushing a wheelbarrow loaded with sand on flat ground

questions.explanation: A biceps curl is a classic third-class lever: the fulcrum is the elbow joint, the effort is the biceps insertion on the radial tuberosity, and the load is the weight in the hand; the effort lies between the fulcrum and the load along the lever arm.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: Standing on tip-toe is a second-class lever in the foot.
Nodding at the neck is a first-class lever system.
Pushing a wheelbarrow is a second-class lever, not third.

questions.question_text: Which of the following projectile-motion factors most strongly determines the maximum horizontal range achieved by a thrown javelin under ideal aerodynamic conditions on a flat field?

options.text: Only the colour of the javelin and not its launch parameters
Initial speed of release, angle of release and height of release
The thrower's hairstyle and audience size around the field
Time of day and total number of judges present at event

questions.explanation: For a projectile in the absence of strong wind, horizontal range depends on initial release velocity, release angle (often near 35–40° for a javelin given throwing dynamics) and release height above the landing surface — the three classical biomechanical projectile factors.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: Colour is irrelevant to projectile mechanics.
Hairstyle and audience size do not affect motion physics.
Time of day and judges have no mechanical effect on flight.

questions.question_text: Match each body movement in Column A with its correct anatomical definition listed in Column B and choose the option that shows the right combination.

column_b.text: Movement of a part toward body midline
Decrease in joint angle between two bones
Increase in joint angle between two bones
Movement of a part away from body midline

questions.explanation: Flexion decreases joint angle (Q); extension increases joint angle (R); abduction moves a part away from the midline (S); adduction moves it toward the midline (P) — the four basic body movements at synovial joints.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: Flexion always reduces the joint angle by definition.
Abduction is movement away from the midline of body.
Adduction brings a body part toward the midline.

questions.question_text: Match each anatomical plane in Column A with the example movement that primarily takes place in that plane from Column B and choose the correct combination.

column_b.text: Spinning of a discus thrower around vertical axis
Forward kick of a football, knee flexion-extension
Side-bending of trunk to the right
Trunk rotation of a batsman during a cover drive

questions.explanation: Sagittal-plane movement is exemplified by knee flexion-extension during a kick; frontal-plane movement by side-bending of the trunk; transverse-plane movement by trunk rotation in batting; whole-body spinning of a discus thrower turns about the long vertical axis.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: Sagittal-plane motion is most common in straight kicks.
Side-bending occurs in the frontal plane only.
Trunk rotation in batting takes place in the transverse plane.

questions.question_text: Match each lever class in Column A with the most appropriate example from the human body or sports activity listed in Column B and choose the correct combination shown.

column_b.text: Biceps curl flexing the elbow joint
Nodding the head at the atlanto-occipital joint
Pushing a loaded wheelbarrow on flat ground
Standing on the balls of the feet (calf raise)

questions.explanation: First-class — head nodding at the atlanto-occipital joint; second-class — calf raise standing on the balls of the feet, where the load is between fulcrum and effort; third-class — biceps curl with effort between fulcrum and load; the wheelbarrow is the classic second-class analogy.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: Calf raise places load (body weight) between fulcrum and effort.
The biceps curl always represents the third-class lever.
The wheelbarrow is the textbook second-class analogy.

questions.question_text: Read the assertion and reason about lowering the centre of gravity in sport and choose the option that best describes their logical relationship in stability biomechanics.
questions.assertion: Wrestlers and judo players adopt a low crouched stance to maximise their balance during competitive bouts.
questions.reason: Lowering the centre of gravity reduces the height through which the line of gravity must move outside the base of support to topple them.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are true; Reason correctly explains Assertion
Both are true but Reason does not correctly explain Assertion
Assertion is true but Reason is false
Assertion is false but Reason is true

questions.explanation: A lower centre of gravity sits closer to the base of support, so an opponent must rotate the body further to push the line of gravity outside that base; this directly improves resistance to toppling, explaining the crouched stance of wrestlers and judo players.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: The reason gives the correct biomechanical mechanism.
Both statements are widely supported by evidence.
A low CG genuinely improves stability against pushes.

questions.question_text: Examine the assertion and reason about projectile motion in sport and choose the option that best represents their logical connection in performance analysis.
questions.assertion: When the take-off and landing heights are equal and air resistance is ignored, a release angle of about 45° gives the maximum horizontal range for a projectile.
questions.reason: At a 45° release angle the horizontal and vertical velocity components are equal, optimising the time in air and horizontal travel together.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are true; Reason correctly explains Assertion
Both are true but Reason does not correctly explain Assertion
Assertion is true but Reason is false
Assertion is false but Reason is true

questions.explanation: Under equal heights and no air resistance, range R = (v² sin 2θ) / g, which is maximised when sin 2θ = 1, i.e. θ = 45°; at this angle the equal horizontal and vertical components together optimise both flight time and horizontal speed.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: The 45° optimum holds only when heights are equal.
The reason correctly identifies the velocity-component balance.
The mathematics directly supports both statements.

questions.question_text: Consider the assertion and reason concerning planes of motion in sport biomechanics and choose the option that best describes their logical relationship.
questions.assertion: A discus thrower's spinning movement during the throw mainly takes place in the transverse plane of the body around its long axis.
questions.reason: The transverse plane divides the body into front and back halves while the long axis runs from head to foot.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are true; Reason correctly explains Assertion
Both are true but Reason does not correctly explain Assertion
Assertion is true but Reason is false
Assertion is false but Reason is true

questions.explanation: The assertion is true — discus spinning is transverse-plane motion around the long axis; however the reason is false because the transverse plane divides the body into upper and lower halves, while the frontal plane divides it into front and back portions.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: Transverse plane divides upper from lower body, not front/back.
Discus spinning is correctly transverse-plane motion.
The reason gives the wrong description of the transverse plane.

questions.question_text: Arrange the following stages of a basic biomechanical analysis of a single sports skill in the correct logical order normally followed by sports biomechanists.

items.text: Identify and define the skill or movement
Provide feedback to coach and athlete
Record the movement using video or sensors
Analyse the data using mechanical principles

options.text: P → R → S → Q
Q → P → R → S
S → R → Q → P
R → P → Q → S

questions.explanation: A typical biomechanical analysis identifies the skill of interest, records the movement using video or sensors, analyses the data using mechanical principles like forces and angles, and finally feeds findings back to the coach and athlete to refine technique.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: Skill identification logically precedes data capture.
Recording must occur before analysis can take place.
Feedback is the closing step of the analysis cycle.

questions.question_text: Arrange the following phases of projectile motion of a long-jumper from take-off to landing into the correct chronological order observed during the jump in athletics.

items.text: Maximum height of centre of gravity reached
Take-off with optimal release angle and velocity
Descending flight toward the landing pit
Landing with feet forward in the sand pit

options.text: Q → P → R → S
P → Q → S → R
S → R → P → Q
R → P → Q → S

questions.explanation: A long-jumper takes off with optimal angle and velocity, climbs to a peak height where vertical velocity becomes zero, descends under gravity toward the pit, and finally lands with feet thrust forward to maximise the measured horizontal distance.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: Take-off is always the initial flight event.
Maximum height precedes the descending phase.
Landing is always the final phase of projectile motion.

questions.question_text: State whether the following statement is true or false: A larger base of support and a lower centre of gravity together generally improve the stability of an athlete's posture.

options.text: True
False

questions.explanation: Stability rises with three factors — a larger base of support, a lower centre of gravity, and the line of gravity falling well within the base; combining the first two factors makes a posture significantly harder to topple by external force.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: Wider stance widens the base, improving stability.
Crouching lowers CG, also improving stability.
Both factors are well-established in mechanics.

questions.question_text: Study the diagram comparing the three classes of mechanical levers in the human body and identify the lever class in which the effort always lies between the fulcrum and the load.

options.text: First-class lever
Second-class lever
Third-class lever
Fourth-class lever

questions.explanation: In a third-class lever the effort lies between the fulcrum and the load; this arrangement gives less mechanical advantage in terms of force, but produces greater speed and range of motion at the load end — the typical pattern of body movement levers.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: First-class places fulcrum between effort and load.
Second-class places load between effort and fulcrum.
There is no recognised fourth-class lever in standard mechanics.
